title: Parachute Drop Air Resistance Lab

effectTimelines.label: Descent animation
effectTimelines.description: Payload falls with visible drag and canopy stretch.

steps.title: Frame the question
steps.prompt: How does parachute canopy size affect the time it takes a payload to reach the ground?
steps.guidance: Choose the comparison that matches the variable you want to test.

options.label: How does canopy size affect descent time?
How does air resistance change when the canopy gets larger?

steps.title: Plan the fair test
steps.prompt: Select the variables and controls for your drop test.
steps.guidance: A fair test changes one feature and keeps the release height and payload mass the same.

options.label: Release from the same starting point

steps.title: Predict with force ideas
steps.prompt: Predict which canopy will stay in the air longest and explain your reasoning.
steps.guidance: Connect your explanation to air resistance and balanced forces.
steps.sentenceStem: I predict ___ will descend most slowly because ___.

steps.title: Run the drop
steps.prompt: Select a canopy size and drop the parachute from the tower.
steps.guidance: Watch the canopy stretch and the payload speed change as it falls.

steps.title: Record the result
steps.prompt: Record the descent time and describe what the parachute did during the fall.
steps.guidance: Use the visual drop and the timing readout together.

steps.title: Compare the trials
steps.prompt: What pattern do you see when canopy size changes?
steps.guidance: Look for a relationship between the larger canopy and the time in the air.

steps.title: Conclude from evidence
steps.prompt: Explain how air resistance affected the parachute’s motion.
steps.guidance: Use the trial timings and the movement you observed.

steps.title: Evaluate the investigation
steps.prompt: What would you improve before running more parachute drops?
steps.guidance: Think about repeated trials and timing precision.

teacherNotes: The scene exaggerates canopy motion slightly to make force changes legible to pupils.
Encourage discussion of when forces become more balanced during the descent.
safetyNote: The tower and release are virtual; real drop tests should be supervised and carried out in a safe open area.
misconceptionChecks: Heavier-looking objects do not always fall faster when air resistance changes.
A larger canopy increases drag; it does not reduce gravity.

hero.blurb: Drop a procedural parachute from a tower and watch drag change the fall in real time.
